title: American Civil War begins with the attack on Fort Sumter
summary: At 4:30 in the morning of Friday 12 April 1861, a mortar shell arched from the Confederate battery at Fort Johnson across the mouth of Charleston Harbor and burst above Fort Sumter, a masonry pentagon holding a Union garrison of eighty-five soldiers under Major Robert Anderson. After thirty-four hours of continuous bombardment from more than forty guns and mortars ringing the harbor, Anderson surrendered on 13 April. No one had been killed by enemy fire, but the shots had opened a civil war that would kill more than seven hundred thousand Americans and end slavery in the United States.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
The election of Abraham Lincoln, the Republican candidate opposed to the extension of slavery into the territories, on 6 November 1860 was received in the Deep South as an unbearable threat. South Carolina, whose planters had led every previous secession crisis from the nullification controversy of 1832 onward, called a state convention that voted unanimously on 20 December 1860 to secede from the Union. Mississippi, Florida, Alabama, Georgia, Louisiana and Texas followed by 1 February 1861; delegates met at Montgomery, Alabama, on 4 February, adopted a provisional constitution and inaugurated the former United States senator Jefferson Davis as president of the Confederate States of America on 18 February. The Confederacy claimed all federal property within its borders.

Most of that property surrendered without a struggle. Federal customs houses, arsenals, post offices, forts and navy yards from Charleston to Galveston were seized by state militias, often with the connivance of the outgoing James Buchanan administration. Only a handful of coastal forts held out, most importantly Fort Pickens at Pensacola in Florida and Fort Sumter at Charleston. Sumter was a five-sided brick fortress on an artificial granite island in the middle of the harbor, planned to mount 135 guns and hold a garrison of 650 but still ninety per cent complete when the crisis broke. Its commander, Major Robert Anderson, a Kentuckian by birth and slaveholder by inheritance but a career United States army officer, held it with two artillery companies, a band of musicians and forty-three civilian workmen, in all around 127 men.

On the night of 26 December 1860, six days after South Carolina's secession, Anderson had moved his garrison in rowboats from the vulnerable Fort Moultrie on Sullivan's Island to the stronger position of Sumter, spiking Moultrie's guns behind him. Carolinian authorities protested that Buchanan had promised to leave Sumter unoccupied. Buchanan, weakly, sent the unarmed merchant steamer Star of the West with two hundred reinforcements on 5 January 1861. Confederate batteries in the harbor fired on her from Morris Island on 9 January and she withdrew without landing troops. Anderson, having no orders to fire, held his ground. Through the winter South Carolina and after March the Confederate government built rings of batteries on Sullivan's Island, Morris Island, James Island and Fort Johnson, mounting some forty-three guns and mortars trained on the fort. By early April Confederate general Pierre Gustave Toutant de Beauregard, a former engineer officer and former pupil of Anderson at West Point, commanded around six thousand troops around the harbor. Anderson's supplies dwindled to a few weeks.

sections.heading: What happened
sections.body:
Lincoln, inaugurated on 4 March 1861, had promised in his address to 'hold, occupy and possess' federal property but not to invade the seceded states. On 4 April he ordered a relief expedition under Captain Gustavus Vasa Fox to sail from New York with provisions but no reinforcements. On 6 April he formally notified South Carolina Governor Francis Pickens by a State Department messenger that the fort would be provisioned only, with no additional men or arms landed unless the resupply was resisted. The decision transferred the moral choice of aggression to the Confederacy. Jefferson Davis's cabinet met at Montgomery on 9 April; only Secretary of State Robert Toombs objected, warning that firing on the fort would be 'suicide, murder, and will lose us every friend at the North'. Davis nevertheless ordered Beauregard to demand the fort's surrender before Fox's ships arrived.

At 3:45 on the afternoon of 11 April three Confederate aides, including former US senator James Chesnut, arrived by boat at Sumter and delivered Beauregard's ultimatum. Anderson refused but remarked that he would be starved out within a few days in any case. His comment was relayed to Beauregard, who around midnight sent his aides back to negotiate a peaceful evacuation date. Anderson agreed to leave the fort by noon on 15 April unless he received new orders or supplies. Chesnut, judging the answer inadequate given the imminent arrival of the relief fleet, wrote out on the spot at 3:20 in the morning of 12 April that Beauregard's batteries would open fire in one hour.

At 4:30 a.m. a signal mortar shell was fired from Fort Johnson on James Island. It burst above Sumter. The Iron Battery on Morris Island fired the first shotted gun; a Virginia secessionist named Edmund Ruffin, aged sixty-seven, was permitted the honour of pulling the lanyard. The ring of batteries opened in a counterclockwise sequence at two-minute intervals to conserve ammunition. Anderson did not return fire for two and a half hours, waiting for daylight. When Captain Abner Doubleday fired the first Union shot at seven a.m., manpower shortages, damaged casemates and a shortage of cloth cartridge bags reduced the fort to firing only six of its guns. Confederate hot shot, iron balls heated red in furnaces on the shore, set the fort's wooden buildings ablaze on the afternoon of 12 April. Rain during the night doused the fires. Fox's relief ships, arriving offshore on 12 April, could not cross the bar in the heavy swell and remained spectators.

On 13 April fires broke out again, the main gate burned, the flagpole was cut down by a Confederate shot at one p.m. and rehoisted on a jury-rigged mast. At two p.m. former Texas senator Louis T. Wigfall, acting on his own initiative, rowed under a white flag from Morris Island and negotiated a suspension of firing. Formal terms were signed that afternoon. On the morning of Sunday 14 April, Anderson's garrison assembled on the parade ground for a fifty-gun salute to the Stars and Stripes. Halfway through the salute a pile of cartridges exploded, killing Private Daniel Hough of the 1st US Artillery outright and mortally wounding Private Edward Galloway. They were the first military dead of the American Civil War. The garrison then marched out with colours flying to the tune of 'Yankee Doodle' and boarded the steamer Isabel for the North.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
The reaction in the North was immediate and unanimous. On 15 April Lincoln issued a proclamation calling on the states to supply 75,000 militia for ninety days' service to suppress the rebellion; Northern governors and legislatures responded with overwhelming offers of manpower. Ohio's governor William Dennison told Washington that his state could raise the full quota by itself. In the border slave states, however, Lincoln's call for troops decided the question of loyalty. Virginia's convention, which had voted against secession earlier in April, reversed itself on 17 April and took the state out of the Union; Arkansas seceded on 6 May, Tennessee on 8 June, North Carolina on 20 May. Missouri's governor Claiborne Jackson refused to supply men for what he called an unholy crusade; his replacement by unionists led to open warfare in the state. Kentucky adopted a policy of armed neutrality that broke down in September when Confederate forces occupied Columbus.

The expanded eleven-state Confederacy moved its capital to Richmond, Virginia, only a hundred miles from Washington. The war's early campaigns of 1861 were exploratory and often incompetent: the First Battle of Bull Run on 21 July, a Union defeat only some thirty miles from the capital, dispelled expectations that the war would be quick. Both sides settled into the immense task of mobilising, training and equipping mass volunteer armies. By 1862 hundreds of thousands were in the field and battles at Shiloh, Antietam and Fredericksburg produced casualty lists on a European scale. The Emancipation Proclamation, issued on 22 September 1862 and taking effect 1 January 1863, transformed the war's meaning from preservation of the Union into a war for the destruction of slavery. Some two hundred thousand black men, most of them formerly enslaved, would eventually serve in the Union armed forces.

The war's turning points came in the summer of 1863 with Union victories at Vicksburg on 4 July and Gettysburg on 1 to 3 July. Ulysses S. Grant, promoted to general-in-chief in March 1864, coordinated a strategy of continuous pressure on all fronts; William Tecumseh Sherman's march through Georgia and the Carolinas in 1864 and 1865 broke the Confederate interior. Robert E. Lee surrendered the Army of Northern Virginia to Grant at Appomattox Court House on 9 April 1865. Six days later Lincoln was shot at Ford's Theatre by John Wilkes Booth and died on 15 April, four years to the day after his call for volunteers. Final Confederate surrenders followed through the spring. Some 620,000 to 750,000 soldiers had died, more Americans than in every other war combined until Vietnam.

The Thirteenth Amendment, ratified 6 December 1865, abolished slavery throughout the United States; the Fourteenth in 1868 and the Fifteenth in 1870 conferred citizenship and voting rights on the formerly enslaved. Reconstruction of the defeated South continued until the compromise of 1877 withdrew the last federal troops from the region.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
The bombardment of Fort Sumter is the founding event of the American Civil War, and by extension of the modern American nation. The compromises over slavery that had held the Republic together since 1787 broke down completely in the four years that followed; the war produced a stronger federal state, a national currency, a national income tax and the constitutional amendments that reshaped citizenship in the country. Every subsequent debate over federal versus state power, over race and civil rights, over the meaning of the Constitution and the Declaration of Independence, has been fought in the shadow of the war that began at Charleston.

Fort Sumter itself changed hands twice more during the war and was heavily bombarded by Union forces during the long siege of Charleston from 1863 to 1865. Reduced almost to rubble, it was repaired and made a coast defence installation once again. In 1948 it became a National Monument administered by the National Park Service; the modern fort, still crowned by a coastal defence battery installed in the Spanish-American War, is one of the most visited historic sites in the American South.

The war's memory has always been contested. Northern veterans remembered it as the war to save the Union and to free the slaves; Confederate veterans and the postwar Lost Cause movement recast it as a war of states' rights and Southern honour, downplaying the centrality of slavery. Monuments to Confederate leaders erected during the Jim Crow era and again in the 1950s and 1960s became the focus of intense controversy in the twenty-first century; many were removed after 2015 and again after the murder of George Floyd in 2020. Debate over the display of the Confederate battle flag, over the naming of military bases and over the interpretation of the war in school curricula continues.

Internationally, the American Civil War was the first great war of the industrial age. It was fought with railways, telegraphs, ironclad warships, rifled artillery, repeating rifles and mass conscript armies. It anticipated in almost every technical particular the wars of European unification and, at greater remove, the world wars of the twentieth century. Military observers from every European army watched its campaigns; its lessons in logistics, medical care, mobilisation and total war passed into the professional literature of the general staffs. And its outcome, the survival of a democratic federal republic, provided in the age of Bismarck and Napoleon III a rare demonstration that popular government could withstand a serious internal war and emerge stronger.

worldContext.text: The Kingdom of Italy was proclaimed on 17 March 1861 under Victor Emmanuel II of Piedmont, the culmination of the Risorgimento; Cavour, its architect, died in Turin on 6 June. Britain and France officially recognised the belligerency of the Confederacy on 13 and 14 May 1861 but not its independence, and both governments watched the American conflict with intense interest. Alexander II of Russia issued the emancipation manifesto freeing twenty-three million serfs on 3 March 1861, the greatest single act of legal liberation in nineteenth-century Europe.

The Qing dynasty was in the exhausted aftermath of the Second Opium War, concluded by the Convention of Peking in October 1860, and still fighting the Taiping Rebellion whose armies threatened Shanghai. The Xianfeng Emperor died on 22 August 1861 in his refuge at Chengde; his young son Tongzhi succeeded him under the regency of the empress dowagers Cixi and Ci'an. In Japan, the shogunate under Tokugawa Iemochi faced growing violence from anti-foreign samurai; the murder of American diplomat Henry Heusken in Edo in January 1861 was one of many attacks on Western residents.

British India, under direct crown rule since 1858, was in the middle of the reforms that followed the great rebellion of 1857 to 1858. The Indian Councils Act of 1861 established legislative councils for the provinces and admitted a small number of Indians to advisory positions. The Indian Penal Code, drafted by Thomas Babington Macaulay, came into force on 1 January 1862. The Indian High Courts Act of 1861 consolidated the courts of Bombay, Madras and Calcutta. Cotton exports to Britain surged as the Union blockade of the Confederacy cut off American supplies.

Egypt under the khedive Said Pasha was in the middle of the construction of the Suez Canal, using labour drawn from the villages of the Nile Delta; Said died on 18 January 1863 and was succeeded by Isma'il Pasha, who continued the project. Lebanon endured a bloody sectarian civil war between Maronite Christians and Druze in 1860 that drew a French military expedition and led to the creation of the special autonomous province of Mount Lebanon in 1861. Sultan Abdulaziz succeeded his brother Abdulmecid on the Ottoman throne on 25 June 1861.

In West Africa the British consolidated their presence on the Gold Coast; Lagos was annexed as a Crown Colony on 6 August 1861. French forces under Louis Faidherbe continued their expansion up the Senegal River. In the Nile valley the explorers John Hanning Speke and James Augustus Grant were on the expedition of 1860 to 1863 that would confirm Lake Victoria as the source of the Nile. In southern Africa the Boer republics of the Orange Free State and the Transvaal were engaged in wars with the Basotho of King Moshoeshoe I.

The First Taranaki War in New Zealand had ended in an inconclusive truce in March 1861; deeper conflict between the settler government and the Maori King Movement was building toward the Waikato invasion of 1863. In Australia, gold rushes at Lambing Flat in New South Wales in June 1861 produced anti-Chinese riots. The Victorian expedition of Robert O'Hara Burke and William John Wills across the Australian interior reached the Gulf of Carpentaria in February 1861 but both explorers died on the return journey.

figures.name: Abraham Lincoln
figures.role: Sixteenth president of the United States, inaugurated on 4 March 1861; issued the call for 75,000 volunteers after Sumter's fall

figures.name: Jefferson Davis
figures.role: President of the Confederate States of America who authorised the demand for Sumter's surrender

figures.name: Major Robert Anderson
figures.role: United States army officer commanding the Union garrison at Fort Sumter

figures.name: P.G.T. Beauregard
figures.role: Confederate general commanding the batteries around Charleston Harbor and directing the bombardment

figures.name: Edmund Ruffin
figures.role: Virginia agriculturalist and secessionist who fired one of the first shotted rounds against Sumter

figures.name: Gustavus V. Fox
figures.role: Former US naval officer, later Assistant Secretary of the Navy, commanding the relief expedition off Charleston bar

images.caption: Bombardment of Fort Sumter, Charleston Harbor, 12 and 13 April 1861. Engraving by George Edward Perine, 1863, for a Civil War publication.
images.credit: George Edward Perine (1837 to 1885), via Wikimedia Commons and New York Public Library

sources.title: Fort Sumter and Fort Moultrie National Historical Park
